title: Matrix Handling
text: function filterMissing deletes taxa /characters containing missing data. following example, output file saved test_filterMissing_FILTERED.nexus: function filterInvariants deletes invariant characters, useful accelerate graph searches. Maximum Likelihood Bayesian analyses using MKv model ascertainment bias correction (ASC), invariants must deleted. , follow definition invariant IQ-Tree, characterized : (1) constant sites containing single character state sequences, (2) partially constant sites (N /-), (3) ambiguously constant sites (e.g. C, Y -). following example, 122 invariants detected.
code: library(RNODE)  filterMissing(input="../testdata/test_filterMissing.nexus",                input_format="nexus",               output_path="../testdata/test_filterMissing",               missing="both") filterInvariants(input="../testdata/015_MORPH_data.nexus",                  input_format = "nexus",                  output_index="../testdata/015_MORPH_data")

title: Matrix Handling
text: function filterSharedTaxa filters two molecular morphological matrices taxon overlap. parameter level controls whether output keeps shared taxa, shared taxa plus taxa unique input 1, shared taxa plus taxa unique input 2. taxon retained one input, missing row matrix filled ?. one input matrices contains protein data output TNT, filterSharedTaxa writes TNT header nstates 32.
code: # Keep only taxa shared by the two matrices filterSharedTaxa(input1="../testdata/013_MORPH_data.tnt", input1_format="tnt",                  input2="../testdata/013_MOL_data.tnt", input2_format="tnt",                  output_path="../testdata/013_TE_data2.tnt",                  output_concatenate=TRUE,                  level="strictly_shared")  # Keep shared taxa plus taxa unique to input1 filterSharedTaxa(input1="../testdata/013_MORPH_data.tnt", input1_format="tnt",                  input2="../testdata/013_MOL_data.tnt", input2_format="tnt",                  output_path="../testdata/013_TE_unique1.tnt",                  output_concatenate=TRUE,                  level="shared+unique1")  # Keep shared taxa plus taxa unique to input2 filterSharedTaxa(input1="../testdata/013_MORPH_data.tnt", input1_format="tnt",                  input2="../testdata/013_MOL_data.tnt", input2_format="tnt",                  output_path="../testdata/013_TE_unique2.tnt",                  output_concatenate=TRUE,                  level="shared+unique2")

title: Matrix Handling
text: function concatenate merges two phylogenetic matrices column-wise. Input can NEXUS TNT files (format auto-detected file extension) matrices already loaded R. Taxa present one inputs filled ? output. instance, using data set Whitcher et al. (2025), concatenate morphological matrix (77 characters, 11 taxa) molecular matrix (45,165 characters, 28 taxa) total-evidence matrix (45,242 characters, 28 taxa): output file 059_TE_data.tnt contains character blocks (& [num] morphology, & [num] molecular) separated TNT block headers. 17 taxa absent morphological matrix automatically padded ? first 77 characters. result can also captured R object without writing file:
code: concatenate(input1       = "../testdata/059_MORPH_data.tnt",             input2       = "../testdata/059_MOL_data.tnt",             output_file  = "../testdata/059_TE_data.tnt",             output_format = "tnt") mat <- concatenate(input1 = "../testdata/059_MORPH_data.tnt",                    input2 = "../testdata/059_MOL_data.tnt")

title: Matrix Handling
text: function splitOrdFromUnord splits morphological matrix partitions ordered unordered characters based list ordered characters. function splitNoStates splits characters morphological matrix according number character-states. procedure recommended run phylogenetic analyses MK MKv models.
code: # Data input of list of ordered characters list_ordered=c(1, 6, 7, 8, 10, 12, 13, 14, 17, 19, 23, 26, 31, 35, 41, 44, 45, 48, 51, 54, 55, 68, 71, 72, 92, 94, 96, 102, 105, 108, 109, 128, 129, 130, 131, 132, 135, 142, 144, 152, 153, 193)  splitOrdFromUnord(input="../testdata/048_MORPH_data.nex",                    input_format = "nexus",                   output_index = "../testdata/048_MORPH",                    list_ordered=list_ordered) splitNoStates(input = "../testdata/015_MORPH_data.nexus",               input_format = "nexus",               output_index = "../testdata/015_MORPH_data",               ambiguity_addState = T,               inapplicable_addState = T,               log=T,               write=T)

title: Matrix Handling
text: functions nexus2tnt tnt2nexus allow easily convert matrices NEXUS TNT formats. specifically designed preserve data type headers (like &[dna] &[prot]) ordered character indices (ccode +), often lost using generic tree-handling libraries. Ambiguities also converted appropriately (e.g. (01) [01] TNT).
code: # Convert NEXUS to TNT nexus2tnt(input_file = "../testdata/048_MORPH_data.nex",           output_file = "../testdata/048_converted.tnt")  # Convert TNT to NEXUS tnt2nexus(input_file = "../testdata/059_MORPH_data.tnt",           output_file = "../testdata/059_converted.nexus")

title: Tree Comparisons
text: Using simple simulations, can demonstrate compare support values trees. first simulate two trees containing support values: Next, run sharedNodes identify matching clades descendants support values. Additionally, also can plot trees.   Alternatively, can identify plot unique clades:
code: library(phytools) set.seed(44) # Simulate tree a a = pbtree(n=7)  # Generate random support values as integers to tree a node_labels = sample(1:100, a$Nnode, replace = TRUE)  # Add the support values as node labels to tree a a$node.label = node_labels   set.seed(88) # Simulate tree b b = pbtree(n=7) # Generate random support values as integers to tree b node_labels = sample(1:100, b$Nnode, replace = TRUE)  # Add the support values as node labels to tree b b$node.label = node_labels library(RNODE) # Compare shared clades and support values (and plot) df = sharedNodes(tree1=a, tree2=b, composition=T,                   plotTrees = T,                  output.tree1="example1.1_simulated1.pdf",                  output.tree2="example1.1_simulated2.pdf",                   tree.width = 3, # adjust tree width                  tree.height = 4, # adjust tree height                  tree.fsize = 1, # adjust font size                  tree.adj=c(1.2,3), # adjust support position                  tree.cex=.5, # adjust support size                  node.numbers=T) # show node index uniqueNodes(a, b, composition=T, dataframe=T,             plotTrees=T, output.tree = "example1.1_unique.pdf",             node.numbers=T,              tree.fsize=2, # adjust text size             tree.cex=7.5, # adjust circle size             sup.adj1=c(-.2,4), # adjust support from tree a             sup.adj2=c(1.3,4) # adjust support from tree b             )

title: Tree Comparisons
text: can use sharedNodes compare two empirical trees .nwk format estimated TNT. Polytomies input trees different taxon samples accepted names corresponding leaves equal input trees. instance, using data set Whitcher et al. (2025), can plot relationship bootstrap values molecular (MOL) total evidence (TE) trees analyzed TNT.  expected, significant correlation bootstrap values MOL TE trees (Spearman: rho = 0.89; P < 0.001).
code: library(ggplot2) # Load trees MOL = read.tree("../testdata/051b_MOL_BS_TNT.nwk") TE = read.tree("../testdata/051d_TE_BS_TNT.nwk")  # Run sharedNodes df = sharedNodes(tree1=MOL, tree2=TE, spearman = T)  # Plot the relationship of support between trees ggplot(df, aes(as.numeric(Support_Tree_1), as.numeric(Support_Tree_2))) +   geom_point(size = 5, show.legend = F, alpha=.5) +   theme_minimal() +    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = T, color = "red", linewidth = .5) +   labs(x="\n Bootstrap in the MOL tree",        y="Bootstrap in the TE tree \n")

title: Tree Comparisons
text: user wants test support values one tree predict occurrence clades another tree, function retrodictNodes creates dataframe containing support values tree 1 occurrence clade tree 2, can used logistic regressions. Using data set Janssens et al. (2018), logistic regression revealed intercept 0.009 (.e. bootstrap 0 first tree, odds presence clade second tree 0.009; P < 0.01). Furthermore, every one-unit increase bootstrap first tree, odds presence clade second tree increase 1.075 (7.5%).
code: # Load trees MOL = read.tree("../testdata/001_MOL_IQTREE.contree") TE = read.tree("../testdata/001_TE_ASC_IQTREE.contree")  # Run retrodictNodes df = retrodictNodes(MOL, TE) df$occurrence_tree2 = as.factor(df$occurrence_tree2)  # Fit the logistic regression model <- glm(occurrence_tree2 ~ support_tree1, data = df, family = binomial) summary(model)  # Convert log-odds to odd ratios exp(coef(model))

title: Tree Comparisons
text: addition descendants support values, branch lengths can compared.  expected, significant correlation bootstrap values MOL TE trees (linear model: estimate = 0.78; R-squared = 0.86; P < 0.001).
code: # Compare branch lengths RNODE::compareBranchLength(MOL, TE, composition=T)  # Correlation between branch lengths summary(lm(data=df, formula=EdgeLength_tree1 ~ EdgeLength_tree2))  # Plot  ggplot(df, aes(as.numeric(EdgeLength_tree1), as.numeric(EdgeLength_tree2))) +   geom_point(size = 5, show.legend = F, alpha=.5) +   theme_minimal() +    geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = T, color = "red", linewidth = .5) +   labs(x="\n Branch lengths in the MOL tree",        y="Branch lengths in the TE tree \n")

title: Tree Comparisons
text: addition comparisons shared clades, support values branch lengths, popular method compare phylogenies based topological metrics. Popular metrics like Robinson-Foulds Cluster Information distances can summarized using summaryTopologicalDist. Moreover, common topological metric number SPR moves edit one tree another tree. However, implementations lacking R normalize SPR distances using refined upper bound Ding et al. (2011) (normalizedSPR) computing SPR distances multiple trees (multiSPR). normalized SPR 0.1931574.
code: # Read trees mol = read.tree("../testdata/003_MOL_IQTREE.contree") te = read.tree("../testdata/003_TE_ASC_IQTREE.contree")  # RF and CID summaryTopologicalDist(mol, te)  # Normalized SPR normalizedSPR(mol, te)

title: Tree Manipulation
text: Given tree without support values (e.g. strict consensus optimal trees) tree B support values (e.g. majority consensus bootstrap pseudo-replicates), mapSupport returns tree support values shared clades tree B. instance, strict consensus optimal trees majority consensus tree bootstrap trees share 223 clades, presenting 6 unique clades strict consensus 1 unique clade bootstrap tree.
code: library(RNODE) library(ape)  # Read trees opt = read.tree("../testdata/051a_strictConsensus_MOL_TNT_results.nwk") BS = read.tree("../testdata/051b_MOL_BS_TNT.nwk")  # Compute topological distances summaryTopologicalDist(opt, BS)  # Map the BS values from the majority consensus tree to the optimal tree opt_with_bs = mapSupport(opt, BS) opt_with_bs[1]

title: Tree Manipulation
text: Finally, can map branch lengths strict consensus either using minimum values pool MPTs randomly selecting one MPTs. Using empirical data Nakamura et al. 2025, demonstrate function mapBranchLength:
code: strict = read.tree("../testdata/cymb_IP_GB.1.nwk") mpts = read.tree("../testdata/cymb_IP_trees.nwk")  # Map using minimum branch length per shared edge mapped_min <- mapBranchLength(strict, mpts, method = "minimum")  # Plot strict + mapped side by side png("../man/figures/example4.2.png", width = 2400, height = 4000, res = 150) par(mfrow = c(1, 2),            # 1 row, 2 columns     mar = c(4, 4, 2, 2),        # margins     oma = c(1, 1, 1, 1))        # outer margins # Panel 1 — strict consensus plot(ladderize(strict),      main = "Strict Consensus Tree",      cex = 0.8) # Panel 2 — mapped branch lengths plot(ladderize(mapped_min),      main = "Mapped Branch Lengths (Minimum)",      cex = 0.8) dev.off()

what: RNODE • R Tools for Phylogenetic Analyses
title: RNODE • R Tools for Phylogenetic Analyses
text: RNODE R package facilitate pre- postprocessing phylogenetic analyses, including (1) comparisons topologies, branch lengths, support values, (2) comparison DNA sequences, (3) manipulation cladistic matrices, (4) manipulation trees. Copyright (C) Daniel Y. M. Nakamura 2026

title: RNODE • R Tools for Phylogenetic Analyses
text: use RNODE, please cite repository.

title: RNODE • R Tools for Phylogenetic Analyses
text: RNODE tested R. v. 4.5.2 can installed following command:
code: devtools::install_github("dnakamuraz/RNODE")

title: RNODE • R Tools for Phylogenetic Analyses
text: following functions available RNODE: following examples designed users little experience. questions, send message using GitHub issues.

title: RNODE • R Tools for Phylogenetic Analyses
text: detailed tutorials examples use functions, please refer package vignettes available website’s Articles section: Tree Comparisons: Identifying shared unique clades, support comparisons, logistic regressions, branch lengths, topological distances. Matrix Handling: Morphological filtering, shared taxa filtering, concatenating matrices, formatting conversions (TNT / NEXUS). Tree Manipulation: Mapping support values branch lengths across different trees.

title: addREP — addREP
text: Given one alignment one tree Goodman-Bremer (GB) support values, compute ratio explanatory power (REP) using equally weighted parsimony.

title: addREP — addREP
code: addREP(data, tree)

title: addREP — addREP
text: data cladistic matrix. tree phylo object can loaded using ape::read.tree NEWICK files TreeTools::ReadTntTree TNT files.

title: addREP — addREP
text: Grant & Kluge (2007, 2010) proposed REP new measure optimality-based support, support values follow rank order GB. REP advantage scaling GB values maximum GB, making REP comparable across datasets. REP calculated : $$REP = \frac{S' - S}{X - S} = \frac{\mathrm{GB}}{\mathrm{GB}_{\max}}$$ S optimal length, S' length tree without given clade, X length worst tree. maximum value GB can calculated using least parsimonious binary tree (obtained searching characters weighted -1), allow groups worst tree contradict groups best tree.

title: addREP — addREP
text: Grant, T., & Kluge, . G. (2007). Ratio explanatory power (REP): new measure group support. Molecular Phylogenetics Evolution, 44(1), 483-487. Grant, T., & Kluge, . G. (2010). REP provides meaningful measurement support across datasets. Molecular Phylogenetics Evolution, 55(1), 340-342.

title: addREP — addREP
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: compareBranchLength — compareBranchLength
code: compareBranchLength(   tree1,   tree2,   write = NULL,   outgroup = NULL,   root = NULL,   composition = F,   unique = F )

title: compareBranchLength — compareBranchLength
text: tree1 phylo object without branch lengths tree2 phylo object branch lengths write Optional. Specify name dataframe file written locally (nothing written parameter specified) outgroup Optional. Specify outgroup taxa remove (default, outgroup = F assumes user want remove outgroup taxa) root Optional. Specify root trees, recommended facilitate tree comparisons (default, root = F assumes trees share root) composition Optional. Specify composition corresponding clades present dataframe (default, composition = F) unique Optional. Show unique clades (default: unique = F)

title: compareBranchLength — compareBranchLength
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: compareBranchLength — compareBranchLength
code: # Example 1 (identify unique nodes) tree1 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t3,(t2,(t4,t5))));") tree2 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t2,(t3,(t4,t5)47)53)94);") compareBranchLength (tree1, tree2) #> All required parameters provided. #> Error in data.frame(ParentNode = tree$edge[, 1], ChildNode = tree$edge[,     2], EdgeType = edge_type, Clade = sapply(clades, paste, collapse = ","),     EdgeLength = tree$edge.length, stringsAsFactors = FALSE): arguments imply differing number of rows: 8, 0

title: concatenate — concatenate
text: Concatenate two phylogenetic matrices (morphological molecular) columns.   Inputs can local NEXUS TNT files, matrices already loaded R.   Taxa present one matrix filled missing data ('?').   input format data types (morphology, DNA, protein) detected automatically   explicitly defined headers.   Also reports translates ordered (additive) characters safely output matrix arrays.

title: concatenate — concatenate
code: concatenate(   input1,   input2,   input1_format = NULL,   input2_format = NULL,   output_file = NULL,   output_format = NULL )

title: concatenate — concatenate
text: input1 First input: local file path (NEXUS TNT) matrix/data.frame already loaded R. input2 Second input: local file path (NEXUS TNT) matrix/data.frame already loaded R. input1_format Format input1 file: 'nexus' 'tnt'. Detected automatically NULL (default). input2_format Format input2 file: 'nexus' 'tnt'. Detected automatically NULL (default). output_file Output file path (e.g. "testdata/059_TE_data.tnt"). NULL, result returned R object . output_format Output format: 'nexus' 'tnt'. NULL, inferred extension output_file ('.tnt' -> tnt, otherwise nexus). Ignored output_file NULL.

text: Invisibly returns concatenated matrix.

title: filterInvariants — filterInvariants
text: Delete invariant characters morphological matrix. follow definition invariant site IQ-Tree: (1) constant sites containing single character state sequences, (2) partially constant sites (N /-), (3) ambiguously constant sites (e.g. C, Y -).

title: filterInvariants — filterInvariants
code: filterInvariants(   input,   input_format = NULL,   output_format = NULL,   output_index = NULL )

title: filterInvariants — filterInvariants
text: input Input file (molecular morphological matrix loaded locally already loaded matrix object R). input_format load local file: 'nexus', 'tnt', NULL auto-detect. Default NULL. output_format write local file: 'tnt', 'nexus', NULL (default; returns R object). output_index Output index (e.g. user specify "Desktop/Index", output files "Desktop/Index_onlyVARIANTS.nexus"). Required output_format NULL.

title: filterSharedTaxa — filterSharedTaxa
text: Filter two molecular morphological matrices shared unique taxa (rows). Can output concatenated separated matrices NEXUS TNT format.

title: filterSharedTaxa — filterSharedTaxa
code: filterSharedTaxa(   input1,   input1_format = NULL,   input2,   input2_format = NULL,   output_path = NULL,   output_format = NULL,   output_concatenate = TRUE,   return_as_list = FALSE,   level = "strictly_shared" )

title: filterSharedTaxa — filterSharedTaxa
text: input1 First input file (molecular morphological matrix nexus, fasta, tnt format) already loaded matrix object R. input1_format load local file: 'nexus', 'fasta', 'tnt'. Defaults NULL (auto-detects). input2 Second input file (molecular morphological matrix nexus, fasta, tnt format) already loaded matrix object R. input2_format load local file: 'nexus', 'fasta', 'tnt'. Defaults NULL (auto-detects). output_path Output path. NEXUS output, "Desktop/Index" output "Desktop/Index_SHARED.nexus" (concatenated) "Desktop/Index_SHARED_1.nexus" "Desktop/Index_SHARED_2.nexus" (separated). TNT output, "Desktop/Index" output "Desktop/Index.tnt" (concatenated) "Desktop/Index_SHARED_1.tnt" "Desktop/Index_SHARED_2.tnt" (separated). NULL, returns matrices list without writing files. output_format Output file format: 'nexus' 'tnt'. NULL (default), format inferred output_path: paths ending .tnt write TNT; others write NEXUS. output_concatenate Logical. TRUE (default), concatenates filtered matrices. FALSE, returns separately. return_as_list Logical. TRUE, returns result list matrices instead writing file. Default FALSE. level Taxon filtering level. Use 'strictly_shared' keep taxa shared input1 input2, 'shared+unique1' keep shared taxa plus taxa unique input1, 'shared+unique2' keep shared taxa plus taxa unique input2. Missing data taxa absent one matrix filled '?'.

title: filterSharedTaxa — filterSharedTaxa
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: filterSharedTaxa — filterSharedTaxa
code: # Example with concatenated NEXUS output filterSharedTaxa(   input1 = "testdata/file1.nexus", input1_format = "nexus",   input2 = "testdata/file2.nexus", input2_format = "nexus",   output_path = "testdata/shared", output_concatenate = TRUE ) #> Warning: cannot open file 'testdata/file1.nexus': No such file or directory #> Error in file(con, "r"): cannot open the connection  # Example with concatenated TNT output (protein data) filterSharedTaxa(   input1 = "testdata/013_MORPH_data.tnt", input1_format = "tnt",   input2 = "testdata/013_MOL_data.tnt", input2_format = "tnt",   output_path = "testdata/013_TE_data", output_format = "tnt",   output_concatenate = TRUE, level = "strictly_shared" ) #> Warning: cannot open file 'testdata/013_MORPH_data.tnt': No such file or directory #> Error in file(con, "r"): cannot open the connection  # Example with separated output filterSharedTaxa(   input1 = "testdata/file1.nexus", input1_format = "nexus",   input2 = "testdata/file2.nexus", input2_format = "nexus",   output_path = "testdata/shared", output_concatenate = FALSE ) #> Warning: cannot open file 'testdata/file1.nexus': No such file or directory #> Error in file(con, "r"): cannot open the connection

title: filterTaxaList — filterTaxaList
text: Filter molecular morphological matrix keeping deleting specific taxa based provided list.

title: filterTaxaList — filterTaxaList
code: filterTaxaList(   input,   input_format = NULL,   list,   filter = "keep",   output = NULL,   output_format = NULL )

title: filterTaxaList — filterTaxaList
text: input local file path (molecular morphological matrix nexus tnt format) already loaded matrix object R. input_format load local file: 'nexus', 'tnt', NULL. Defaults NULL (auto-detects extension). list local .txt file path R object (vector, list, data.frame) containing list terminals, one terminal name per row/element. filter Character string. Use 'delete' remove taxa contained list, 'keep' retain taxa contained list. output Output path (e.g., "Desktop/Filtered"). NULL, returns matrix R object without writing files. output_format Output file format: 'nexus', 'tnt', NULL. NULL, format inferred `output` extension, simply returns R object `output` also NULL.

title: filterTaxaList — filterTaxaList
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: filterTaxaList — filterTaxaList
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ # Keep only the taxa specified in a text file filterTaxaList(     input = "testdata/matrix.tnt",     list = "testdata/taxa_to_keep.txt",     filter = "keep",     output = "testdata/filtered_matrix.tnt" )  # Delete taxa using an R vector and return as an object taxa_to_remove <- c("TaxonA", "TaxonB") filtered_mat <- filterTaxaList(     input = "testdata/matrix.nexus",     list = taxa_to_remove,     filter = "delete" ) } # }

title: mapBranchLength — mapBranchLength
text: tree1 phylo object without branch lengths trees2 phylo object branch lengths method. Optional. Method sample branch lengths. "random", randomly select one trees trees2. "minimum" (default), map minimum values pool MPTs (trees2) edge present tree1

title: mapBranchLength — mapBranchLength
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: mapBranchLength — mapBranchLength
code: # Example 1 (identify unique nodes) tree1 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t3,(t2,(t4,t5))));") tree2 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t2,(t3,(t4,t5)47)53)94);") mapBranchLength (tree1, tree2) #>  #> Phylogenetic tree with 5 tips and 4 internal nodes. #>  #> Tip labels: #>   t1, t3, t2, t4, t5 #>  #> Rooted; includes branch length(s).

title: mapSupport — mapSupport
text: Given tree 1 without support values (e.g. strict consensus optimal trees) tree 2 support values (e.g. majority consensus suboptimal bootstrap pseudo-replicates), return tree 1 support values shared clades tree 2.

title: mapSupport — mapSupport

title: mapSupport — mapSupport

title: multiCID — multiCID
text: multiCID computes CID distances two sets binary trees (e.g. MPTs), \(T_1 = \{\text{Tree}_1, \text{Tree}_2, \dots, \text{Tree}_n\}\) \(T_2 = \{\text{Tree}_a, \text{Tree}_b, \dots, \text{Tree}_z\}\). methods available (1) randomly selecting one binary trees set (quick naive) (2) estimating mean CID (minimum maximum) \(n\) pairwise combinations two sets. trees must contain set leaves.

title: multiCID — multiCID
code: multiCID(   trees1,   trees2,   method = "random",   normalization = FALSE,   subsample = 1 )

title: multiCID — multiCID
text: trees1 phylo multiPhylo object multiple trees can loaded using ape::read.tree NEWICK files TreeTools::ReadTntTree TNT files. pool MPTs presents binary non-binary trees, binary trees processed. trees2 Another phylo multiPhylo object. method Optional. Specify CID distances calculated (1) random (default: selects one binary tree randomly multiPhylo object), (2) meanCID (calculates mean pairwise CID distances two multiPhylo objects), (3) minCID (calculates minimum), (4) maxCID (calculates maximum), (5) (calculates mean, minimum, maximum values). normalization Optional. Specify CID distances normalized. default, CID distances normalized. subsample Optional. Specify CID distances calculated fraction total number trees available set (default = 1; .e. trees evaluated). Zero values accepted.

title: multiCID — multiCID
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: multiRF — multiRF
text: multiRF computes Robinson-Foulds distances two sets binary trees (e.g. MPTs), \(T_1 = \{\text{Tree}_1, \text{Tree}_2, \dots, \text{Tree}_n\}\) \(T_2 = \{\text{Tree}_a, \text{Tree}_b, \dots, \text{Tree}_z\}\). methods available (1) randomly selecting one binary trees set (quick naive) (2) estimating mean RF (minimum maximum) \(n\) pairwise combinations two sets. trees must contain set leaves.

title: multiRF — multiRF
code: multiRF(   trees1,   trees2,   method = "random",   normalization = FALSE,   subsample = 1 )

title: multiRF — multiRF
text: trees1 phylo multiPhylo object multiple trees can loaded using ape::read.tree NEWICK files TreeTools::ReadTntTree TNT files. pool MPTs presents binary non-binary trees, binary trees processed. trees2 Another phylo multiPhylo object. method Optional. Specify RF distances calculated (1) random (default: selects one binary tree randomly multiPhylo object), (2) meanRF (calculates mean pairwise RF distances two multiPhylo objects), (3) minRF (calculates minimum), (4) maxRF (calculates maximum), (5) (calculates mean, minimum, maximum values). normalization Optional. Specify RF distances normalized using maximum possible RF distance \((T_1) + (T_2)\) (sum clades trees). default, RF distances normalized. subsample Optional. Specify RF distances calculated fraction total number trees available set (default = 1; .e. trees evaluated). Zero values accepted.

title: multiRF — multiRF
text: Daniel YM Nakamura

title: multiSPR — multiSPR
text: multiSPR computes SPR distances two sets binary trees (e.g. MPTs), \(T_1 = \{\text{Tree}_1, \text{Tree}_2, \dots, \text{Tree}_n\}\) \(T_2 = \{\text{Tree}_a, \text{Tree}_b, \dots, \text{Tree}_z\}\). methods available (1) randomly selecting one binary trees set (quick naive) (2) estimating mean SPR value (minimum maximum) \(n\) pairwise combinations two sets. function useful two strict consensus trees exhibit polytomies. trees must contain set leaves.

title: multiSPR — multiSPR
code: multiSPR(   trees1,   trees2,   method = "random",   normalization = FALSE,   subsample = 1 )

title: multiSPR — multiSPR
text: trees1 phylo multiPhylo object multiple trees can loaded using ape::read.tree NEWICK files TreeTools::ReadTntTree TNT files. pool MPTs presents binary non-binary trees, binary trees processed. trees2 Another phylo multiPhylo object. method Optional. Specify SPR distances calculated (1) random (default: selects one binary tree randomly multiPhylo object), (2) meanSPR (calculates mean pairwise SPR distances two multiPhylo objects), (3) minSPR (calculates minimum value), (4) maxSPR (calculates maximum value), (5) (calculates mean, minimum, maximum values). normalization Optional. Specify SPR distances normalized using upper bound values (Ding et al. 2011). See details normalizedSPR. default, SPR distances normalized. subsample Optional. Specify SPR distances calculated fraction total number trees available set (default = 1; .e. trees evaluated). Zero values accepted.

title: multiSPR — multiSPR
text: Ding, Y., Grünewald, S., Humphries, P.J., 2011. agreement forests. J. Comb. Theory Ser. 118(7), 2059–2065.

title: normalizedSPR — normalizedSPR
text: normalizedSPR computes normalized SPR distances two binary trees set leaves. trees polytomies, see multiSPR.

title: normalizedSPR — normalizedSPR
code: normalizedSPR(tree1, tree2, method = "Ding", outgroup = NULL, root = NULL)

title: normalizedSPR — normalizedSPR
text: tree1 phylo object can loaded using ape::read.tree NEWICK files TreeTools::ReadTntTree TNT files. tree2 Another phylo object method Optional. Select method calculation upper bound values: "traditional" "Ding" (default). See section Details. outgroup Optional. Specify outgroup taxa remove (default, function assumes user want remove outgroup taxa). root Optional. Specify root trees, necessary make SPR distances meaningful (default, function assumes trees share root).

title: normalizedSPR — normalizedSPR
text: SPR distance two trees \(T_1\) \(T_2\) \(n\) leaves defined minimum number SPR moves required convert one tree (Goloboff 2008). calculation SPR distances NP-hard different heuristic procedures available (e.g. Nakhleh et al. 2005; Beiko Hamilton 2006; Goloboff 2008; Oliveira Martins 2008). SPR distance popular topological distance metric measure incongruence two trees. However, SPR distances compared across datasets (e.g. Torres et al. 2021), SPR distances may coupled number leaves thus precluding statistical comparisons. Thus, normalization values ranging zero one taking account \(n\) required. Traditionally, upper bound SPR distances \(n-3\). However, Ding et al. (2011) proposed refined upper bound SPR distances trees \(n>=4\): $$SPR_{\text{upper bound}} = n - 3 - \left(\frac{\sqrt{n - 2} - 1}{2}\right)$$ Thus, given SPR distance (calculated TreeDist::SPRDist) upper bound Ding et al. (2011), normalized SPR distance : $$SPR_{\text{normalized}} = \frac{SPR_{\text{distance}}}{SPR_{\text{upper bound}}}$$

title: normalizedSPR — normalizedSPR
text: Beiko, R.G., Hamilton, N., 2006. Phylogenetic identification lateral genetic transfer events. BMC Evol Biol. 6, 15. Ding, Y., Grünewald, S., Humphries, P.J., 2011. agreement forests. J. Comb. Theory Ser. 118(7), 2059–2065. Nakhleh, L., Ruths, D., Wang, L.-S., 2005. RIATA-HGT: fast accurate heuristic reconstructing horizontal gene transfer. : Proceedings Eleventh International Computing Com- binatorics Conference (COCOON 05). Lecture Notes Computer Science. LNCS . 3595, Springer. pp. 84–93. Goloboff, P.., 2008. Calculating SPR distances trees. Cladistics 24(4), 591–597. Oliveira Martins, L., Leal, E., Kishino, H., 2008. Phylogenetic detection recombination Bayesian prior distance trees. PLoS One 3(7), e2651. Torres, ., Goloboff, P.., Catalano, S.., 2021. Assessing topological congruence among concatenation-based phylogenomic approaches empirical datasets. Mol. Phylogenet. Evol. 161, 107086.

title: normalizedSPR — normalizedSPR
text: Daniel YM Nakamura, WC Wheeler, T Grant

Width trees PDF plotTrees = T. tree.height Optional. Height trees PDF plotTrees = T. tree.fsize Optional. Font size PDF plotTrees = T. tree.adj Optional. Adjust horizontal vertical position plotTrees = T. tree.cex Optional. Adjust support size nodes plotTrees = T. tree2.direction Optional. Adjust direction tree2 ("leftwards" vs "rightwards") plotTrees = T. output.tree1 Optional. output file name tree 1 plotTrees = T. output.tree2 Optional. output file name tree 2 plotTrees = T. tanglegram Optional. Plot tanglegram minimizing number crosses lines linking two trees PDF format. input tree branch length, uniform lengths simulated enable visualization. output.tangletree Optional. output file name tanglegram = T. tanglegram.margin Optional. Mean support = 53.5" #> [1] "Support of shared clades in tree 1: 32\342\200\22375 (53.5)" #> [1] "Tree 2: Total number of clades = 3 ; Mean support = 50" #> [1] "Support of shared clades in tree 2: 47\342\200\22353 (50)" #>   Node_Tree_1 Node_Tree_2 Support_Tree_1 Support_Tree_2 Composition_Tree_1 #> 1           5           5                               (((t4,t5),t3),t1); #> 2      ((t4,t5),t3); #> 3           (t4,t5);  # Example 3 (plot  two trees) sharedNodes (tree1, tree2, plotTrees=T, tree.width=8, tree.height=8, tree.fsize=3, tree.adj=c(-1.5,0.5), tree.cex=0.6) #> All required parameters provided. #> [1] "" #> [1] "Tree comparisons done!" #> [1] "Number of shared clades:  3" #> [1] "Tree 1: Total number of clades = 3 ; title: splitNoStates — splitNoStates
text: Splits morphological matrix according number character-states. procedure recommended run phylogenetic analyses MK MKv models ('K' refers number states). Khakurel et al. (2024) demonstrated MK models high K values can underestimate branch lengths, whereas MK models small K values can overstimate . , recent studies partitioned morphological characters according number states (e.g. Černý & Simonoff 2023).

title: splitNoStates — splitNoStates
code: splitNoStates(   input,   input_format = NULL,   ambiguity_addState = FALSE,   inapplicable_addState = FALSE,   output_index = "output",   log = FALSE,   write = TRUE )

title: splitNoStates — splitNoStates
text: input Input file (morphological matrix, either loaded previously R loaded local file). ambiguity_addState T, ambiguities counted additional character-states (default: ambiguity_addState = F). inapplicable_addState T, inapplicable states counted toward sum unique character-states. output_index Output index (e.g. user specify "Desktop/Index", output files "Desktop/Index_ORDERED.nexus" "Desktop/Index_UNORDERED.nexus") log T, write file locally reporting destination character. write T, write files locally. inpu_format load local file: 'nexus' 'tnt'.

title: splitNoStates — splitNoStates
text: Černý, D., & Simonoff, . L. (2023). Statistical evaluation character support reveals instability higher-level dinosaur phylogeny. Scientific Reports, 13(1), 9273. Khakurel, B., Grigsby, C., Tran, T. D., Zariwala, J., Höhna, S., & Wright, . M. (2024). fundamental role character coding Bayesian morphological phylogenetics. Systematic biology, 73(5), 861-871.

Write TSV file current directory containing output dataframe (default, dataset = T). dataframe1.name Optional. Name write dataframe 1. dataframe2.name Optional. Name write dataframe 2. plotTrees Optional. Plot two trees taxa pruning PDF format. plot = T, user also adjust PDF dimensions (e.g. width = 8, height = 8), label size (e.g. fsize = 4), position size support values (e.g. adj = c(-1.5,0.5), cex = 0.6). node.numbers Optional. plotTrees = T, show node index (confuse support values'default, True). tree.width Optional. Width trees PDF plotTrees = T. tree.height Optional. Height trees PDF plotTrees = T. tree.fsize Optional. Font size PDF plotTrees = T. tree.adj Optional. Adjust horizontal vertical position plotTrees = T. tree.cex Optional. Adjust support size nodes plotTrees = T. sup Optional. Optional. Plot support values (default, sup = T). sup.adj1 Optional. sup = T, adjust horizontal vertical position support values tree1. sup.adj2 Optional. sup = T, adjust horizontal vertical position support values tree2. sup.cex Optional. sup = T, adjust font size support values tree1. output.tree Optional. output file name tree 1 plotTrees = T.

title: uniqueNodes — uniqueNodes
text: Daniel YM Nakamura, Taran Grant

title: uniqueNodes — uniqueNodes
code: # Example 1 (identify unique nodes) tree1 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t2,(t3,(t4,t5)75)32)45);") tree2 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t6,(t3,(t4,t5)47)53)94);") uniqueNodes (tree1, tree2) #> All required parameters provided. #> [1] "Both trees with support values." #> character(0) #> character(0) #> [[1]] #> [1] Node    Support #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names) #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] Node    Support #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names) #>   # Example 2 (count unique nodes) tree1 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t2,(t3,(t4,t5)75)32)45);") tree2 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t6,(t3,(t4,t5)47)53)94);") nrow (uniqueNodes (tree1, tree2, composition = F)) #> All required parameters provided. #> [1] "Both trees with support values." #> character(0) #> character(0) #> NULL  # Exmple 3 (highlight unique nodes in plotting) tree1 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t2,(t3,(t4,t5)75)32)45);") tree2 = read.tree (text="(t1,(t6,(t3,(t4,t5)47)53)94);") uniqueNodes (tree1, tree2, plotTrees=T, node.numbers=F, tree.width=14, tree.height=17, tree.fsize=0.8, tree.adj=c(-1.5,0.5), tree.cex=2) #> All required parameters provided. #> [1] "Both trees with support values." #> character(0) #> character(0) #> [[1]] #> [1] Node    Support #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names) #>  #> [[2]] #> [1] Node    Support #> <0 rows> (or 0-length row.names) #>

title: RNODE 0.3.0
text: Added nexus2tnt tnt2nexus format conversion functions robustly handle data type headers ordered characters. Updated pkgdown website use Bootstrap 5 “flatly” theme. Restructured documentation extracting large README examples separate vignette articles (“Tree Comparisons”, “Matrix Handling”, “Tree Manipulation”). Added NEWS.md track changes updates.
